title: NDCG and MRR for Retrieval
concept: Rank-aware metrics that reward relevant documents appearing earlier in the retrieved list.

exercises.prompt: What does the metric recap: What does Precision@5 measure? Precision@5 measure in a search system?

options.text: It measures the speed of the retrieval engine.
It measures what fraction of the top 5 retrieved documents are actually relevant.
It measures if the first result is always the absolute best one.

exercises.explain: Precision@5 measures the fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant within the top 5 results, without considering their specific order.

cards.title: Does Order Matter?
cards.body: When you search for something online, you rarely look past the first few results. If a search engine finds the perfect answer but buries it at the bottom of the page, standard precision counts it as a success, but to a user, it is a failure.

cards.title: What are Rank-Aware Metrics?
cards.body: Rank-aware metrics like MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) and NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) measure not just IF relevant documents are retrieved, but WHERE they appear. The higher up a relevant document is, the higher the score.

cards.title: The Podium Analogy
cards.body: Imagine search results are like a leaderboard in a race. precision@K only checks if your favorite runners finished in the top 5, but NDCG and MRR actually care who got the gold, silver, and bronze medals!

cards.title: Rank in Action
cards.body: If a user searches for 'how to bake bread', and the best recipe is at position 1, MRR is 1.0. If that perfect recipe slips to position 4, the MRR drops to 0.25, reflecting a worse user experience.

cards.title: The Order Blindspot
cards.body: You might think a system with 100% precision@5 is always perfect. However, if all the relevant documents are clustered at positions 4 and 5 instead of 1 and 2, the user still has to scroll, which is why we need ranking metrics.

cards.title: Good vs. Bad Rank Position
cards.body: Let's compare two search result pages. Even though both find the exact same number of relevant documents, the order makes a huge difference to the user.

left.text: Perfect result is at Position 1. The user gets their answer instantly without scrolling.

right.text: Perfect result is at Position 10. The user has to scroll past useless links to find it.

exercises.prompt: How is the Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) calculated for a single search query?

options.text: It averages the positions of all documents.
It calculates 1 divided by the rank of the first relevant document.
It counts only the documents that the user clicked on.
It ignores the order of results completely.

exercises.explain: MRR focuses entirely on the single first relevant document found. Its score is calculated as 1 divided by the rank of that first correct result.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The maximum possible score for NDCG is 1.0, representing the ideal ranking order.
exercises.explain: NDCG scores are normalized so that they always fall between 0.0 and 1.0, where 1.0 represents the perfect possible ranking of those documents.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how ranking metrics evaluate search results.
exercises.sentence: Rank-aware metrics reward systems that place relevant documents earlier in the list.
exercises.explain: Rank-aware metrics apply a discount to documents that appear later, meaning they reward systems that put relevant documents earlier in the list.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes the purpose of NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain)?

options.text: NDCG is a sorting algorithm that reorganizes databases to save storage space.
NDCG stands for New Document Category Generator, which automatically writes titles.
NDCG measures search quality by penalizing highly relevant documents that appear lower down in the results.

exercises.explain: Option C represents a typical AI-generated response describing NDCG, highlighting how it discounts documents based on their position (rank) to model user behavior. Options A and B are unrelated to ranking metrics.

exercises.prompt: You compare two search engines. System A returns its first relevant document at rank 3. System B returns its first relevant document at rank 2. Based on Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), which system is better and why?

options.text: System B is better because its MRR is 0.5, while System A's MRR is 0.33.
System A is better because it found more total documents.
Both systems are equal because they both found at least one relevant document.
System B is better because its NDCG is guaranteed to be 1.0.

exercises.explain: MRR looks only at the rank of the first relevant document (1st is 1.0, 2nd is 0.5, 3rd is 0.33). Since System B found its first relevant document at rank 2 (reciprocal rank of 0.5), it outperforms System A which found its first relevant document at rank 3 (reciprocal rank of 0.33).
